title: Health Care Flexible Spending Account (FSA) Optimization

requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must elect participation in employer's health care FSA to pay qualified medical expenses with pre-tax dollars while carefully estimating expenses to minimize forfeiture under use-or-lose rules
criteria: Employer must offer health care FSA as part of IRC §125 cafeteria plan
Maximum contribution for 2025: $3,200 per employee (indexed annually)
Eligible expenses include medical, dental, vision expenses qualifying under IRC §213(d) not reimbursed by insurance
Election made during annual enrollment; generally cannot change mid-year except qualifying life events
Expenses must be incurred during plan year (definition based on plan design - incurred when service provided)
Claims submitted during plan year plus run-out period (typically 60-90 days after plan year end)
Plan may offer $640 carryover (20% of $3,200) OR 2.5 month grace period, not both
Cannot contribute to general purpose health care FSA if participating in HSA; must use limited purpose FSA

health_care_fsa_rules.annual_limit_2025: $3,200 maximum employee contribution; employer may contribute additional amounts
health_care_fsa_rules.pre_tax_savings_score: Contributions reduce taxable wages saving federal income tax, FICA (7.65%), and often state income tax
health_care_fsa_rules.use_or_lose_rule: Unused amounts forfeited unless plan offers carryover ($640 for 2025) or grace period options
health_care_fsa_rules.uniform_coverage: Full elected amount available first day of plan year even though contributed ratably through payroll
health_care_fsa_rules.limited_purpose_fsa: Can participate with HSA if FSA limited to dental, vision, and post-deductible medical expenses only
health_care_fsa_rules.qualified_expenses: IRC §213(d) expenses including prescriptions, copays, deductibles, dental, vision, certain OTC items

restrictions: Use-or-lose rule creates risk of forfeiture; requires careful expense estimation and year-end planning
Cannot participate in general purpose health FSA and contribute to HSA simultaneously
Carryover and grace period are mutually exclusive; plan can offer one or neither, not both
Election changes generally prohibited mid-year except for qualifying events (marriage, birth, divorce, employment change)
Terminated employees may elect COBRA continuation but must weigh cost against remaining FSA balance
Over-the-counter medications generally require prescription except specific items like menstrual products, first aid supplies
Cosmetic procedures, insurance premiums, and long-term care expenses not qualified for FSA reimbursement
State tax treatment generally follows federal but some states have different rules requiring state tax adjustment
